notes: European Union regulations and directives governing data protection, AI, cybersecurity, and digital services. Regulations apply directly in member states; directives require national implementation. EUR-Lex provides access to EU law. The EU AI Act became effective August 2025.

data.official_name: General Data Protection Regulation
data.description: EU regulation on data protection and privacy (Regulation 2016/679). The global benchmark for data protection law.
data.notes: Effective May 2018. Applies to organizations processing EU residents' data regardless of organization location. Enforced by national Data Protection Authorities (DPAs). Maximum penalties: 4% of annual global turnover or €20M.

children.description: GDPR article reference (e.g., art-32, art-5). Articles are the operative legal provisions.

data.note: EUR-Lex full-text HTML. Per-article anchors not available via stable URL pattern. Third-party sites (gdpr-info.eu) provide per-article pages but are not official EU resources.

examples.note: Security of processing — requires appropriate technical and organizational measures
Principles relating to processing of personal data
Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
Data protection by design and by default

children.description: GDPR chapter or recital reference. Recitals provide interpretive context for articles.

data.note: Chapters group related articles. Recitals are non-binding interpretive guidance that precede the articles in the regulation text. Courts and DPAs frequently cite recitals to interpret article requirements.

examples.note: Recital on appropriate technical and organizational measures (supports art-25 and art-32)

data.official_name: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
data.description: World's first comprehensive AI regulation (Regulation 2024/1689). Risk-based framework classifying AI systems.
data.notes: Effective August 2025 (phased). Bans certain AI practices, sets requirements for high-risk AI systems. Establishes conformity assessment procedures.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full regulation as a single document.

examples.note: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
Transparency obligations for certain AI systems

children.description: AI Act annex reference (Roman numerals). Annexes define technical requirements and high-risk categories.

data.note: Annexes are integral to the AI Act's risk classification system. Annex III lists high-risk AI system areas.

examples.note: Areas of high-risk AI systems (biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, etc.)

data.official_name: Network and Information Security Directive 2
data.description: EU directive on cybersecurity requirements for essential and important entities (Directive 2022/2555).
data.notes: Expands scope beyond NIS1. Requires incident reporting within 24 hours. Applies to energy, transport, banking, health, digital infrastructure. As a directive, requires national transposition — implementation varies across member states.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full directive as a single document.

data.official_name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
data.description: EU regulation on digital operational resilience for financial entities (Regulation 2022/2554).
data.notes: Applies to banks, insurance, investment firms, and their ICT service providers. Effective January 2025. Establishes requirements for ICT risk management, incident reporting, digital operational resilience testing, and third-party risk management.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full regulation as a single document.

data.official_name: Revised Payment Services Directive
data.description: EU directive mandating Strong Customer Authentication (SCA), open banking access for licensed third-party providers (AISPs/PISPs), and enhanced fraud reporting. Transposed into 27 national laws across EU member states.
data.notes: Adopted 2015, effective Jan 2018; SCA enforcement Sept 2019. Underpins the UK OBIE Read/Write API (post-Brexit), Berlin Group NextGenPSD2, STET PSD2 API, PolishAPI, and other European Open Banking specifications. Successor PSD3 / Payment Services Regulation (PSR) proposed 2023.

match_nodes.description: Payment Services Directive 3 / Payment Services Regulation

data.official_name: Payment Services Directive 3 and Payment Services Regulation
data.description: Proposed (2023) successor to PSD2; split into a Directive (PSD3, on licensing) and a Regulation (PSR, on conduct rules). Strengthens SCA, expands open finance scope, addresses payment fraud, and introduces VOP (Verification of Payee) requirements.
data.notes: Legislative proposals published June 2023; expected to apply ~2026-2027 once finalised.

match_nodes.description: eIDAS Regulation (electronic identification and trust services)

data.official_name: Electronic Identification and Trust Services Regulation
data.description: EU regulation establishing a framework for electronic identification, authentication, and trust services (electronic signatures, seals, time-stamps, registered delivery, website authentication) across the single market.
data.notes: Effective July 2016. Provides three Levels of Assurance (LoA) for eID schemes: low, substantial, high. Member states notify their national eID schemes (e.g., Italian SPID, German eID, French FranceConnect+) for cross-border recognition.

data.official_name: Revised eIDAS Regulation establishing the European Digital Identity Wallet
data.description: Updates eIDAS to mandate the EU Digital Identity Wallet (EUDI Wallet) — a sovereign cross-border digital-identity wallet for citizens, residents, and businesses. Member states must offer at least one notified wallet by November 2026.
data.notes: In force May 2024; wallet-rollout deadline 2026. Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) defines the wallet's technical specs and integrates Verifiable Credentials (W3C VC, OpenID4VC).

data.official_name: Cyber Resilience Act
data.description: EU regulation imposing horizontal cybersecurity requirements on products with digital elements (hardware, software, components) placed on the EU market. Establishes essential requirements, conformity assessment, CE marking, and vulnerability handling obligations.
data.notes: Adopted December 2024. Main provisions apply from December 2027 (36 months after entry into force); vulnerability and incident reporting obligations apply earlier from September 2026. Companion to EN 18031 harmonised standard for radio equipment under the Radio Equipment Directive.

data.official_name: Directive on Privacy and Electronic Communications
data.description: EU directive on privacy in electronic communications. Governs cookies, electronic marketing, traffic data, and confidentiality of communications. Pending replacement by an ePrivacy Regulation (long-stalled).
data.notes: Amended significantly by Directive 2009/136/EC (the 'Cookie Directive' amendments). The proposed ePrivacy Regulation has been in legislative limbo since 2017.

data.official_name: Critical Entities Resilience Directive
data.description: Companion directive to NIS2 covering physical and operational resilience of critical entities across 11 sectors (energy, transport, banking, financial market infrastructure, health, drinking water, wastewater, digital infrastructure, public administration, space, food).
data.notes: Adopted December 2022, transposition deadline October 2024.

match_nodes.description: Radio Equipment Directive Article 3(3) cybersecurity requirements

data.official_name: Radio Equipment Directive Article 3(3) cybersecurity essential requirements
data.description: Activation of cybersecurity essential requirements under the Radio Equipment Directive (2014/53/EU) Article 3(3) for internet-connected radio equipment. Mandatory for radio equipment placed on the EU market from 1 August 2025.
data.notes: Harmonised standard for compliance: EN 18031 series.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data
data.description: Establishes rights for users of connected products/IoT devices to access data they generate and share it with third parties, sets conditions for B2B and B2G data sharing, includes safeguards against unlawful third-country government access to non-personal data, and imposes switching obligations on cloud/data-processing service providers.
data.notes: Entered into force January 11, 2024; generally applicable from September 12, 2025.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2022/868 on European data governance
data.description: Creates a framework to increase trust in voluntary data sharing by regulating 'data intermediation services' (neutral brokers connecting data holders and reusers), setting conditions for public-sector bodies to allow reuse of protected data (trade secrets, personal data, IP), and establishing 'data altruism' organisations for voluntary donation of data for the public good.
data.notes: Entered into force June 23, 2022; applicable since September 24, 2023.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 establishing rules for a safer digital space
data.description: Imposes tiered obligations on intermediary/hosting services, online platforms, and especially Very Large Online Platforms/Search Engines (>45M EU monthly users) — covering illegal-content takedown, transparency of recommender systems and ads, risk assessments, and independent audits.
data.notes: Entered into force November 2022; VLOP/VLOSE obligations applied from August 2023, full applicability from February 17, 2024.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 on contestable and fair markets in the digital sector
data.description: Targets designated 'gatekeeper' platforms with ex-ante obligations and prohibitions (interoperability, anti-self-preferencing, data-combination limits) enforced solely by the European Commission, with fines up to 10% (20% for repeat offences) of worldwide turnover.
data.notes: Entered into force November 1, 2022; applicable from May 2, 2023.

match_nodes.description: Cybersecurity Act (ENISA mandate + EU cybersecurity certification framework)

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2019/881 on ENISA and on ICT cybersecurity certification
data.description: Establishes ENISA as a permanent EU agency and creates the EU cybersecurity certification framework — voluntary schemes for ICT products, services, and processes (e.g. EUCC, EUCS in progress).
data.notes: Applicable since June 27, 2019, with authority/penalty/complaint provisions applying from June 28, 2021. See secid:entity/enisa.europa.eu for the agency itself.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2025/38 strengthening solidarity and capacities in the Union to detect, prepare for and respond to cybersecurity threats and incidents
data.description: Establishes a European Cybersecurity Alert System (interconnected National and Cross-Border Cyber Hubs) and a Cybersecurity Emergency Mechanism (stockpiling, incident response support, mutual assistance).
data.notes: Published January 15, 2025; entered into force February 4, 2025.

data.official_name: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on liability for defective products
data.description: Replaces the 1985 Product Liability Directive (85/374/EEC). Extends strict (no-fault) liability to software, AI systems, and digital manufacturing files as 'products', eases the claimant's burden of proof (including for AI's technical complexity), and covers damage from defective updates.
data.notes: Published November 18, 2024; member states must transpose by December 9, 2026.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2023/988 on general product safety
data.description: Replaces Directive 2001/95/EC. Requires consumer products not covered by sector-specific EU law to be safe, addressing risks from digitalisation, connected devices, and cybersecurity as safety factors, with new obligations for online marketplaces.
data.notes: Entered into force June 12, 2023; applicable from December 13, 2024.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2023/1230 on machinery
data.description: Replaces the Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC. Sets health/safety requirements for machinery design and construction, adding explicit cybersecurity obligations — safety-related control systems and software must resist both accidental faults and deliberate cyberattacks, with manufacturers required to provide security updates for up to 10 years.
data.notes: Applies from January 20, 2027.

data.official_name: Regulation (EU) 2025/327 on the European Health Data Space
data.description: Creates an EU-wide legal, technical, and governance framework for electronic health data — giving individuals control over/access to their own health data and enabling secondary use (research, policymaking, innovation) under strict security/governance controls.
data.notes: Entered into force March 26, 2025; primary-use provisions (patient summaries, ePrescriptions) phase in by March 2029.
